2005-06-01.title: Voice of Zarqawi on Tape Is Confirmed
2005-06-01.abstract: American intelligence experts identify voice of Abu Musab al-Zarqawi on 17-minute audio tape in which insurgent leader says he has been wounded slightly but remains in Iraq; on tape, Zarqawi addresses himself to Osama bin Laden as 'soldier to his emir' (S)

2005-06-01.title: Blair to press Bushon Africa development
Opposition to U.S. Makes Chávez a Hero to Many
2005-06-01.abstract: Venezuela Pres Hugo Chavez is emerging as this generation's Fidel Castro, arousing roaring crowds throughout Latin America with his denunciations of United States and American imperialism; Chavez, like Castro, is charismatic figure and self-styled revolutionary who bearhugs his counterparts on state visits, inspires populist left-wing movements and draws out fervent well-wishers from Havana to Buenos Aires; has one great advantage that Castro never had--richest oil reserves outside Middle East, which is providing cash that he is using to weave ever closer diplomatic and commercial ties with Latin American leaders; photos (L)

2005-06-01.title: Dutch Are Expected to Vote No on European Charter Today
2005-06-01.abstract: Dutch are expected to reject European constitution in their referendum on June 1; are unhappy with direction European Union has been taking in recent years, above all since it adopted single currency and expanded eastward, taking in 10 new members; opinion polls show that Dutch feel pushed around by big countries and that union's heavy bureaucracy lacks transparency and democracy and is growing too fast; photo (M)

2005-06-02.title: Gloomily, Israel's top general ends his term
World Briefing | Asia: India: Swift Sentence For 2 Rapists
2005-06-02.abstract: In a rare case of swift judgment, a court in western Rajasthan convicted two men of raping a German tourist in Jodhpur three weeks ago and sentenced them to life in prison. They were arrested two days after the attack, and their trial began almost immediately. The speed of the case was in stark contrast to rape cases that languish in Indian courts.   Hari Kumar (NYT)

2005-06-02.title: French Workers Greet New Prime Minister With Rail Strike
2005-06-02.abstract: French workers greeted the new government of Prime Minister Dominique de Villepin today not with a show of solidarity, but with a national transportation strike.

2005-06-02.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Children Now As Deprived As After War
2005-06-02.abstract: Millions of Russian children are being neglected and their situation is on a par with the desperate post-World War II period, the interior minister, Rashid G. Nurgaliyev, told a meeting of ministry officials. About 700,000 children are orphans, he said, while two million adolescents are illiterate and four million use drugs. He called for more collaboration between law-enforcement agencies and human rights groups and other public organizations.   Sophia Kishkovsky (NYT)

2005-06-03.title: Killing of journalist raises stakes in Lebanon election
Asia powers to cooperate on terrorism
Rumsfeld Urges China to Increase Political Freedom
2005-06-03.abstract: The U.S. defense secretary, arriving in Singapore for a conference on Asian security, drew a sharp distinction between the region's major powers,  China and India.

2005-06-03.title: World Briefing | Europe: Norway: Reward For Munch Paintings
2005-06-03.abstract: Oslo has offered a reward of $329,000 for information leading to the recovery of two Edvard Munch paintings, ''The Scream'' and ''Madonna,'' which were taken in a brazen daylight robbery of the Munch Museum last August. Four men have been jailed in the case, but formal charges have yet to be filed, a police spokesman said. ''We're optimistic the paintings will eventually turn up and hope the reward will shorten the time,'' said the spokesman, Morten Hojem Ervik.   Walter Gibbs (NYT)

2005-06-03.title: Swiss voters likely to say 'yes' to closer EU ties

2005-06-04.title: Bolivian Offers Reform Plan to Protesters Seeking Autonomy
2005-06-04.abstract: Hoping to end mounting nationwide protests, President Carlos Mesa of Bolivia announced plans late Thursday for the election of a citizen assembly to write a new constitution and for a national referendum on greater regional autonomy.    With Congress deadlocked over the two popular demands, and with swarms of protesters paralyzing the country, Mr. Mesa told Bolivians in a nationally televised address that the election and referendum would take place Oct. 16. Political analysts say Congress must ratify the plan, but Mr. Mesa said he had to act fast.

2005-06-04.title: Despite Years of U.S. Pressure, Taliban Fight On in Jagged Hills
2005-06-04.abstract: For weeks, sightings of Taliban fighters were being reported all over the rugged mountains here. But when Staff Sgt. Patrick Brannan and his team of scouts drove into a nearby village to investigate a complaint of a beating, they had no idea that they were stumbling into the biggest battle of their lives.    On May 3, joined by 10 local policemen and an interpreter, the scouts turned up at a kind of Taliban convention -- of some 60 to 80 fighters -- and were greeted by rockets and gunfire. The sergeant called for reinforcements and was told to keep the Taliban engaged until they arrived. ''I've only got six men,'' he remembers saying.

2005-06-04.title: U.S warns of Asian nuclear arms race

2005-06-05.title: U.S. Uncovers Vast Hide-Out of Iraqi Rebels
2005-06-05.abstract: American marines have discovered an elaborate series of underground bunkers used recently by insurgents in central Iraq, with heavy weapons, a kitchen and fresh food, furnished living quarters, showers and even a working air-conditioner, the military said Saturday.    The bunkers were built into an old rock quarry north of the town of Karma, an insurgent stronghold in Anbar Province that lies near the city of Falluja. The bunker system is 558 feet by 902 feet, nearly equal to a quarter of the Empire State Building's office space, making it the largest underground insurgent hide-out to be discovered in at least the past year, if not during the entire war, said Capt. Jeffrey S. Pool, a spokesman for the Second Marine Division.

2005-06-05.title: Hezbollah Is Focus of Voting in Lebanon
2005-06-05.abstract: As campaigns go, the effort to bring out the vote in southern Lebanon is proving more of a battle cry than a national hymn.     Lebanese in the impoverished, predominantly Shiite south will go to the polls on Sunday to elect candidates for 23 seats representing the region in the 128-seat Parliament. The two main Shiite parties, the militant group Hezbollah and the more secular Amal, are widely expected to take almost all the seats.

2005-06-05.title: U.S. troops uncover Iraqi rebel hideout
Hunger for Energy Transforms How India Operates
2005-06-05.abstract: Fed by a decade-long economic boom, India's ever-growing appetite for energy is quietly reshaping the way it operates in the world, changing relations with its neighbors, extending its reach to oil states as far flung as Sudan and Venezuela, and overcoming Washington's resistance to its nuclear ambitions.     Hovering over India's energy quest is its biggest competitor: China, which is also scouring the globe to line up new energy sources. The combined appetite of the two Asian giants is raising oil prices and putting greater demands on world oil supplies.

2005-06-05.title: Brazilian Leader Goes on Defensive as Scandal Spreads
2005-06-05.abstract: A growing corruption scandal has put President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva on the defensive and sapped popular support for his government just as the political maneuvering in advance of the election for president next year is heating up.    With an inquiry now under way in the Brazilian Congress, and likely to drag on for months, normal legislative business has ground to a halt. That paralysis threatens Mr. da Silva's agenda, which includes both tax and labor code changes long sought by business groups and social spending bills to benefit the poor. Mr. da Silva's aides have been trying to win over fence sitters in Congress the old-fashioned way, with generous servings of pork-barrel money.

2005-06-06.title: Briefly: Labor-camp raid finds conditions like slavery
Hungary holds first round of voting to choose new president
Central Asia closes ranks on terrorism
A New Scourge Afflicts Haiti: Kidnappings
2005-06-06.abstract: Haiti is poised for implosion, with almost all its institutions ravaged from inside out by corruption and no one certin who is in charge; chaos reigns; ruthless mobs led by drug traffickers, former military officers and corrupt police officers have set off devastating wave of murders, carjackings, armed robberies and rapes; estimated 6 to 12 kidnappings occur in Port-au-Prince every day; some are high-profile cases, but overwhelming majority seem aimed at middle and working classes; one vegetable vendor was reportedly kidnapped for $30 ransom; most families negotiate with kidnappers on their own, fearful of involving police; human rights groups report that more than 700 people, including seven peacekeepers, have been killed in last eight months; Prime Min Gerard Latortue claims many gang leaders inciting violence are Haitians who have spent time in American prisons; photo of 14-month-old kidnap victim after being reunited with her mother; map (M)

2005-06-06.title: Hezbollah and allies claim victory in southern Lebanon

2005-06-07.title: Car Bombs Kill at Least 20 as Attacks Roil Northern Iraq
2005-06-07.abstract: A week into the government's offensive in Baghdad, insurgents showed their ability to quickly move operations elsewhere.

2005-06-07.title: Bolivian president offers to resign as growing street protests paralyze capital
U.S. supports Europe, NATO chief asserts
In a Spotlight, Baath Congress Opens in Syria
2005-06-07.abstract: President Bashar al-Assad opened a landmark Baath Party congress on Monday, promising modest political reform and calling for a renewed focus on the economy. But the most dramatic change likely to come out of the meeting is the transformation of the party from a pan-Arab movement to a Syrian one.     The changes are likely to emphasize a loose Arab federation and trade connections, delegates said, as well as raise the Syrian character of the party. Instead of creating a single government, the party will seek something more akin to the European Union, said George Jabbour, a member of Parliament.

2005-06-07.title: Sweeping constitutional changes adopted in Taiwan

2005-06-08.title: Swiss riled by EU rule on workers
Poland ties treaty vote to EU summit actions
Protesters keep up La Paz blockade
World Business Briefing | Europe: Poland Adds More Doubt To European Constitution
2005-06-08.abstract: Poland's position on the European Union's constitution was put in doubt as President Aleksander Kwasniewski said that any decision on ratification would depend on the outcome of a meeting of the bloc next week in Brussels. ''At the summit, we may decide to give ourselves a few months and meet again when we are better prepared,'' he said. ''A lack of a decision is also a decision.'' His statements came a day after Britain said it would postpone indefinitely its decision to hold a referendum on the constitution. Mr. Kwasniewski also contradicted his foreign minister, Adam Rotfeld, who said Monday that Poland would press ahead with ratification and suggested an October date.   Judy Dempsey (IHT)

2005-06-09.title: Aides say atomic chief won't heed U.S. on Iran
Israel's Supreme Court rules Gaza withdrawal plan legal
In Iran, election poses a dilemma
Latin States Shun U.S. Plan to Watch Over Democracy
2005-06-09.abstract: In a sharp setback for the Bush administration's Latin America policy, the Organization of American States rejected a United States plan on Tuesday to create a committee to monitor the exercise of democracy in the hemisphere.    Instead, the organization agreed, at a meeting in Fort Lauderdale, Fla., on a declaration that attaches equal or greater emphasis to attacking poverty because of what it calls ''the interdependent relationship of democracy and social and economic development.''

2005-06-09.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Galloway Accuses Labor Of Voter Fraud
2005-06-09.abstract: The police are considering whether to investigate allegations of voter fraud in an east London electoral district. George Galloway, who in May narrowly won the parliamentary seat in the district, Bethnal and Bow, contends that the election was marred by a ''dirty tricks'' campaign by the Labor-controlled local government. Mr. Galloway recently appeared before the United States Senate to defend himself against charges that he profited from the United Nations oil-for-food program in Iraq. A Labor official in Bethnal and Bow said that ''the slurs on the electoral process are disgraceful.''   Sarah Lyall (NYT)

2005-06-10.title: Pope Tells Jews He Will Fight Anti-Semitism
2005-06-10.abstract: In his first major interfaith meeting, Pope Benedict XVI on Thursday assured a delegation of Jewish leaders of the Roman Catholic Church's commitment to fighting anti-Semitism and to closer ties among Jews and Catholics.      ''My predecessors, Pope Paul VI and, in a particular way, Pope John Paul II, took significant steps toward improving relations with the Jewish people,'' he said in a meeting at the Vatican. ''It is my intention to continue on this path.''

2005-06-10.title: Fungus Fatal to Mosquito May Aid Global War on Malaria
2005-06-10.abstract: In a finding that may open promising new ways to attack malaria, scientists are reporting today that two fungi that are harmless to humans and the environment can be used to kill mosquitoes.    The fungi are already licensed in Western countries to control aphids, termites and other pests, according to two studies in the journal Science. One of the researchers, Dr. Matt B. Thomas, a biologist at Imperial College in London, estimated that a ''deliverable product'' could be ready in three to five years, if he could get money for further research.

2005-06-10.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Abbas Calls On Militants To Maintain Calm
2005-06-10.abstract: The Palestinian leader Mahmoud Abbas held talks in Gaza City with rival Palestinian factions, including the radical groups Hamas and Islamic Jihad, and secured a pledge that they would try to maintain the calm between Palestinians and Israelis. But the militants said they reserved the right to retaliate for Israeli attacks. ''We emphasized our right to respond,'' said a Hamas spokesman, Sami Abu Zuhri. The meeting took place as a truce declared in February by Mr. Abbas and Prime Minister Ariel Sharon of Israel appeared to be fraying. In the latest flare-up, Hamas fired a rocket into the town of Sederot, and Israel sent soldiers into the West Bank town of Karbatiya in pursuit of an Islamic Jihad commander, killing him and two others. Islamic Jihad then fired mortars at Jewish settlements, and two Palestinian workers and a Chinese worker were killed.   Christine Hauser (NYT)

2005-06-10.title: World Briefing | Europe: Serbia: Washington Resuming Aid
2005-06-10.abstract: The United States lifted a five-month-old freeze on a $10 million aid package. The resumed aid follows the Serbian government's improved cooperation with the international war crimes tribunal in The Hague. In recent months, the government has helped send 12 suspects to the court. In announcing the aid resumption, R. Nicholas Burns, the under secretary of state for political affairs, who is on a visit to Belgrade, said he believed that Serbia was seriously working to track down one of the tribunal's most wanted fugitives, Ratko Mladic. General Mladic, the former Bosnian Serb military chief, is wanted on charges of genocide in the killing of more than 7,000 Muslims in Srebrenica in 1995.   Nicholas Wood (NYT)

2005-06-11.title: Bush and Roh play down differences on North Korea
Democrats List More Names in Inquiry on Bolton's Access
2005-06-11.abstract: Senate Democrats have prepared a list of approximately three dozen ''names of concern'' and are asking the Bush administration for assurances that John R. Bolton did not misuse his access to highly classified intelligence to seek information about them.    The request is outlined in a letter sent Thursday by Senators Joseph R. Biden Jr. of Delaware and Christopher J. Dodd of Connecticut, two of the leaders of the Democratic opposition to Mr. Bolton's nomination as ambassador to the United Nations. The letter was sent to the senior Republican and Democratic senators on the Intelligence Committee, who have also been involved in negotiations with the Bush administration over access to information about Mr. Bolton's actions when he was an official at the State Department.

2005-06-11.title: U.S. and Seoul Try to Ease Rift on Talks With the North
2005-06-11.abstract: President Bush and South Korea's president, Roh Moo Hyun, tried Friday to shore up an alliance that has shown strains as Washington and Seoul pursue different strategies to deal with North Korea's nuclear weapons program.    Mr. Roh left saying they had brought ''closure'' to some of their differences, but Mr. Bush's public comments suggested that significant disagreements remained.

2005-06-11.title: Ex-official says he has copies of Arroyo tape
Hamilton Naki, 78, Self-Taught Surgeon, Dies
2005-06-11.abstract: Hamilton Naki, laborer who became highly-skilled self-taught surgeon and assisted in world's first human heart transplant, dies at age 78; photo (M)

2005-06-12.title: Videotape of Serbian Police Killing 6 Muslims From Srebrenica Grips Balkans
2005-06-12.abstract: The videotape has brought the pain of that time to the surface for the families of the men killed, but is also having an effect in Serbia.

2005-06-12.title: Iraq Moves Further Toward a Political Stalemate
2005-06-12.abstract: Shiite leaders offered to give Sunnis 15 seats on the constitutional committee, but Sunnis rejected the offer. Also today, Iraqi police found 20 bodies buried in a field.

2005-06-12.title: In Midst of War's Clamor, Music Soothes Iraqi Souls
2005-06-12.abstract: The applause filling the large concert hall after the first swells of Beethoven subsided seemed as much for the audience as for the musicians.    Iraqis packed the red upholstered seats in the darkened hall of the National Theater in central Baghdad on Friday night, while men with machine guns stood in the aisles. Two snipers perched outside on a second-floor balcony. Guards frisked concertgoers as they entered.

2005-06-12.title: At Least 10 Are Killed by Bombs in Iran
2005-06-12.abstract: TEHRAN, Iran, June 12 - Seven bomb blasts shattered Iran's pre-election calm today, leaving at least 10 people dead and nearly 80 wounded in one of the worst outbreaks of political violence here since the end of the Iran-Iraq war in 1988.

2005-06-12.title: Corrections
2005-06-12.abstract: A marginal quotation today on Page 28 of the Book Review, with the review of ''This I Believe: An A to Z of a Life,'' by Carlos Fuentes, misstates a word. As the review notes, Fuentes says, ''Extreme attention is the creative faculty,'' not ''facility.''

2005-06-13.title: 4 bomb attacks kill 8 as Iran readies for vote
Bush and Roh leave key issue open
Iraq Tribunal Releases Tape of Hussein Being Questioned
2005-06-13.abstract: The Iraqi special tribunal questioned Saddam Hussein about the killings of dozens of men in a Shiite village in 1982.

2005-06-14.title: Asia looks at Europe and sees pitfalls
World Briefing | Europe: Expansion Said To Be Off The European Agenda
2005-06-14.abstract: For the first time in years, European Union leaders plan to drop any mention of the bloc's planned expansion to Turkey, Croatia and other countries in their joint statement after a summit meeting this week, Western European diplomats said. The move raised concerns that expansion might become a casualty of the rejection of the European Union constitution by French and Dutch voters. ''We need to give our citizens time to breathe,'' the union's external affairs commissioner, Benita Ferrero-Waldner of Austria, above, told reporters in Luxembourg.   Katrin Bennhold (IHT)

2005-06-14.title: UN nuclear agency reappoints its chief
Sri Lankan leader vows to let rebels help with aid

2005-06-15.title: Pressure rises on Arroyo to talk about audio recordings
Gandhi's Moscow flight raises questions in India
Elder Brother of Ex-President Is Freed in Mexico
2005-06-15.abstract: Raúl Salinas, the elder brother of former President Carlos Salinas, walked out of jail on Tuesday, his murder conviction thrown out by an appeals court. His release marked a personal victory for both brothers. But political analysts said the former president remained an outcast, far from recovering his good name and influence.    Raúl Salinas left the prison outside Mexico City shortly after midnight. With his murky business dealings, expansive estates and a string of rodeo horses, he was once a symbol of the excesses and graft of the long-ruling Institutional Revolutionary Party, or PRI. His conviction on charges of ordering the killing of a powerful political rival was widely considered an example of the party's ruthlessness.

2005-06-15.title: World Briefing | Americas: Guatemala: Villagers Lynch 2 Gang Suspects
2005-06-15.abstract: Villagers in Palinche, about 25 miles from the capital, Guatemala City, tied and beat to death two young men suspected of being members of the feared Mara 18 street gang, one of Central America's biggest, after they sought to blackmail a local merchant on Monday, the federal police reported. There have been no arrests. The police said that the victims' bodies had tattoos identifying them as ''maras,'' or gang members. The authorities have estimated that there are 70,000 to 100,000 gang members across Central America and Mexico.   Antonio Betancourt (NYT)

2005-06-15.title: Briefly: India expresses concern over remark on Kashmir

2005-06-16.title: Chinese writer tests the power of his press
U.S. Says It Has Captured Al Qaeda Leader for Mosul Area
2005-06-16.abstract: The military described Muhammad Khalaf Shakar as Abu Musab al-Zarqawi's most trusted operations agent in Iraq.

2005-06-16.title: Spain says it has cut pipeline to Iraq rebels
Chief of 'Millennium' Aid Fund Is Quitting
2005-06-16.abstract: Two days after a group of African leaders complained that the Bush administration's signature program to aid poor nations had proved slow, the head of the program told his staff on Wednesday that he would resign.    Paul V. Applegarth, the chief executive of the Millennium Challenge Corporation for the last 13 months, will step down at a date yet to be determined, an official there said. The corporation administers the Millennium Challenge Account, established by President Bush to provide financial assistance to poor nations that show progress in establishing what the United States considers stable democratic governments and pursuing sound economic and social welfare policies.

2005-06-16.title: Hong Kong set to appoint next leader

2005-06-17.title: World Briefing | Asia: Sri Lanka: Coalition Partner Quits
2005-06-17.abstract: President Chandrika Kumaratunga's fragile coalition government lost a major partner, the Peoples Liberation Front, over the move to share $2 billion in tsunami aid with Tamil Tiger rebels. With the 39 seats held by members of the Peoples Liberation Front, the coalition had a bare 113 seats in the 225-member Parliament. The loss of those seats, however, is not expected to lead to the immediate collapse of the government because the main opposition party is not calling for a no-confidence vote.   Hari Kumar (NYT)

2005-06-17.title: Bush Aid Initiative for Poor Nations Faces Sharp Budget Cuts and Criticism of Slow Pace
2005-06-17.abstract: With the United States under heavy international pressure to double aid to poor countries, one of President Bush's signature foreign aid initiatives is facing severe cuts in its proposed budget, intense criticism from African leaders and the departure of its director after only a year in the job.     On Thursday, a House subcommittee recommended almost halving the president's $3 billion request for next year, citing the pressures of a tight budget.

2005-06-17.title: In Yukon, Today's Con Men Mix With Gold Rush Ghosts
2005-06-17.abstract: The folks here still roll their own cigarettes, drink hard and gamble in a casino where ragtime is played on a stand-up piano. It has been that way since the great Klondike gold rush a century ago filled Dawson with 30,000 gold-mad miners, dancing girls and an assortment of scoundrels in a matter of months.    These days the town is down to a population of 1,200 and the municipal government is bankrupt despite a growing tourism industry and artists' colony. But some things never change in the remote Yukon.

2005-06-17.title: Syrian Influence Is Seen in Lebanese Parliamentary Vote
2005-06-17.abstract: As the four-week Lebanese elections approach their last round on Sunday, opposition leaders, critics and Western diplomats contend that Syrian intelligence operatives, intertwined over 15 years with the Lebanese security services, are still operating here and influencing the vote despite the withdrawal of Syrian troops and official denials from Damascus.    ''The Syrians are still controlling the army, the security services,'' said a former high-ranking Lebanese intelligence officer, who stipulated that his name could not be used because of the danger of assassination. ''They issue orders by phone, by e-mail, by fax,'' the former officer said. ''Everybody in this government is still manipulated by Syria.''

2005-06-18.title: European Union's Heated Budget Negotiations Collapse
2005-06-18.abstract: A European Union summit meeting ended in failure and mutual recriminations early Saturday morning, after the collapse of negotiations over plans for a budget.    Reflecting the turmoil after a series of political setbacks in recent weeks, leaders of the European Union lashed out at one another. The major obstacle was a standoff between President Jacques Chirac of France and Prime Minister Tony Blair of Britain. Mr. Chirac demanded that Britain give up part of the refund of billions of dollars a year that it has received from the European Union for more than two decades.

2005-06-18.title: North Korea's Leader Says He's Ready to Resume Talks to End Nuclear Standoff
2005-06-18.abstract: North Korea's leader, Kim Jong Il, said Friday that his nation was ready to resume negotiations over its nuclear arms program as early as next month, provided the United States treated it with respect, the South Korean government said.     Mr. Kim also said that if the nuclear crisis were resolved, North Korea would be ready to rejoin the Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty and allow international nuclear inspectors inside the country, said Chung Dong Young, South Korea's minister of unification, who met with Mr. Kim. This part of the proposal was apparently being put forth for the first time, South Korean Foreign Ministry officials said.

2005-06-18.title: Kim Jong Il signals readiness to resume nuclear arms talks
Just Across the River in Iraq, Expatriates Send a Message Home
2005-06-18.abstract: If there is one thing the new Iraq can learn from Iran, Sakina Habi Alok and her husband said, it is how to hold free elections.    So, shortly before noon on this sweltering Friday, Ms. Alok, an Iranian citizen, accompanied her family and another Iranian woman to an elementary school downtown, across the Shatt al Arab waterway from their homeland, to vote in the Iranian elections.

2005-06-18.title: Israel is reported to plan a sea barrier off Gaza

2005-06-19.title: Test of Wills: the Burmese Captive Who Will Not Budge
2005-06-19.abstract: Daw Aung San Suu Kyi will mark her 60th birthday under house arrest, which she has endured since addressing an anti-government rally in 1988.

2005-06-19.title: Suicide Bomber Kills at Least 23 at Baghdad Restaurant
2005-06-19.abstract: The attack targeted a busy restaurant near the heavily fortified Green Zone government compound in Baghdad today.

2005-06-19.title: Seoul Awaits Japan's Chief With Complaints in Mind
2005-06-19.abstract: President Roh Moo Hyun of South Korea and Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi of Japan will hold what is likely to be a tense summit meeting.

2005-06-19.title: Deaths and a Doctor's Past Transfix Australians
2005-06-19.abstract: When Dr. Jayant Patel accepted a surgical post at the municipal hospital here two years ago, it was a big relief to this farm town, which has had trouble finding and keeping doctors. Dr. Patel was a professor of surgery at the State University of New York at Buffalo in the 1980's, and he came highly recommended by several doctors in Oregon, where he had also worked.     But almost immediately, after he declared a patient to be stable when in fact the patient was brain dead, at least one nurse worried about his competence. Before long, nurses were hiding patients from him. One doctor ordered that Dr. Patel not operate on any of his patients, after he saw him perform an operation without anesthesia. An anesthesiologist nicknamed him ''Dr. Death.''

2005-06-19.title: U.S. Troops Press Offensive as Baghdad Diner Bombed

2005-06-20.title: Afghans Arrest 3 Pakistanis Accused of Plot to Kill U.S. Envoy
2005-06-20.abstract: Three Pakistanis planned to assassinate the American ambassador to Afghanistan at a ceremony, Afghan officials said today.

2005-06-20.title: Rival views on EU are out in the open
Suicide car bomber kills at least 20 in Iraq
Iranian Reform Party Hints That It Will Support Insider
2005-06-20.abstract: Reform party of Iran hints that it would throw its support behind former two-term president Ali Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani in runoff presidential vote; proposal, made reluctantly, comes after disputed first-round election cuts reformist candidates out of race and propels untra-conservative mayor or Tehran into second place, behind Rafsanjani; photo (M)

2005-06-20.title: U.S. kills 20 as Talibanmoves to step up attacks

2005-06-21.title: 15 Recruits, Mostly Kurds, Are Killed by Car Bomb
2005-06-21.abstract: Car bomb kills 15 and wounds 125 in Erbil, northern Kurdish town that had until recently been one of most peaceful towns in Iraq; mayor says strike is reminiscent of May 4 strike that killed 60 people; militant group Ansar al-Sunna claimed responsibility for that strike; June 20 bombing in Erbil is one of several leaving total of 30 people dead; two bombs go off near Kirkuk, also in north; in Baghdad, four car bombs explode, killing 10 members of Iraqi security forces; photo (M)

2005-06-22.title: New pay plan (and perks) for EU legislators
A War Shrine, for a Japan Seeking a Not Guilty Verdict
2005-06-22.abstract: Letter from Asia on emotional symbolism of Yasukuni Shrine, Shinto memorial to Japan's war dead; photos; right-wing rallies protest any suggestion, by Japanese or foreigners, that Japanese wars were not legitimate and that war leaders were criminals (M)

2005-06-22.title: Schwarzenegger slips as agenda goes flat
Iraqi Insurgents Refining Bombs; G.I. Deaths Rise
2005-06-22.abstract: American casualties from bomb attacks in Iraq have reached new heights in last two months as insurgents use advanced devices that penetrate armored vehicles and detonate by infrared lasers bypassing electronic jammers; May saw about 700 attacks against US forces using improvised explosive devices, highest number since 2003 invasion; 'shaped' charges also concentrate blast, causing even higher casualties; IEDs of all types caused 33 American deaths in May and at least 35 so far in June; IEDs now account for about 70 percent of American casualties; photo; graph; car bomb attacks against US forces, both suicide attacks and remotely-detonated charges, reached high of 70 in April; senior officials meeting at Fort Irwin grapple with 'very, very dangerous period;' insurgents, especially in rural areas, pack more explosives into huge bombs that, in two recent incidents, killed five marines near Ramadi and five more in Haqlaniya; expertise suggests well-trained bomb-makers, perhaps even rocket scientists from former government (M)

2005-06-22.title: Names of the Dead
2005-06-22.abstract: List of four Americans newly confirmed dead in Iraq; 1,718 American service members have died since start of war

2005-06-23.title: U.S. to give food aid to North Korea
British may gain an ally in Merkel
Serbs suggest Mladic may be nearing arrest
Afghan-U.S. troops continue assault on Taliban
Upstart in Iran Election Campaigns as Champion of Poor
2005-06-23.abstract: Mahmud Ahmadinejad, presidential candidate in Iran, casts himself as simple, religious man who, if elected president, will fight for poor; his critics say he would usher in Taliban-style government and impose strict Islamic values; front-runner Ali Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani is promoting his many years of experience in Iran's government as his credential for election; photo (M)

2005-06-24.title: BBC's New Weather Report: Look, It's Raining Brickbats
2005-06-24.abstract: British Broadcasting Corp's redesign of its weather graphics has proved almost as controversial as question of what 'nice day' in Britain actually is, in terms of weather; company's market research showed viewers found old system boring and confusing, but new design has drawn more than 4,000 official viewer complaints and been denounced in House of Lords, House of Commons and Scottish Parliament; old static map of British isles was replaced by constantly moving picture that causes some viewers to feel queasy; photos (M)

2005-06-24.title: World Briefing | Africa: South Africa: 5 Lawmakers Resign In Fraud Scandal
2005-06-24.abstract: Five legislators of the governing party, the African National Congress, resigned from Parliament after striking plea-bargain agreements on charges of fraud stemming from the misuse of their official travel funds. Twenty-one of Parliament's 450 legislators, mostly from the party, have been accused of diverting the money after an investigation by the national prosecutor. A party spokesman said the resignations were an ''demonstration of remorse'' for wrongdoing and showed the party's zero-tolerance stance on corruption. Opposition politicians were less forgiving, saying that the five legislators were lucky to escape prosecution.   Michael Wines (NYT)

2005-06-24.title: Swiss seek answersafter trains roll to halt
Briefly: Democrats urge Rove to apologize or resign

2005-06-25.title: Customs pact seeks to stiffen security
News Analysis:For Europe, the costs of a weak Germany
U.S. Said to Bar Spanish From Terror Detainee
2005-06-25.abstract: The Bush administration has refused to allow the Spanish authorities to interview a man accused of being an operative of Al Qaeda whose testimony could be crucial to the prosecution of two men on trial here charged with helping to plan the terrorist attacks of Sept. 11, 2001, Spanish officials say.    With little more than a month left in the trial, the chief prosecutor in the case said he was still pressing the request to interview the accused man, Ramzi bin al-Shibh, who is suspected of playing a central role in organizing the attacks.

2005-06-25.title: For Bulgarian voters, worries about EU entry date
Falluja is shut down after 2 marines are killed

2005-06-26.title: Blacksmith's Son Emphasized His Modest Roots
2005-06-26.abstract: In choosing Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, Iranian voters elevated a blacksmith's son who has made more of his modest background and frugal ways than his revolutionary credentials or political experience.

2005-06-26.title: Hesitantly, Congolese Take Initial Step to National Elections
2005-06-26.abstract: Entering the office of the local electoral commission here does not necessarily build confidence that Congo will be ready for the first nationwide elections in this country's history. Voting will begin in the fall and continue through next year.    The office has no phones or computers, and voting lists are still very much a work in progress. The commissioners have worried looks on their faces, and it is easy to understand why.

2005-06-26.title: Riverbank Promenade in Basra Pulses Anew With Life
2005-06-26.abstract: There is a famous Iraqi folk song called ''He Went to Basra and Forgot About Us.'' It tells of love and broken promises and wounds that cannot be healed.    Abdul Raheem Sultan listened to a tape of it on Thursday as he drove the six dusty hours to Basra, his hometown, from Baghdad. He said he had not been back in 20 years, and he wanted to see it one last time before moving his family to Cairo to escape the war.

2005-06-26.title: Suicide bomber kills 6 in northern Iraq

2005-06-27.title: The Rock-and-Roll Saviors
2005-06-27.abstract: Twenty years after the "Live Aid" concerts, Bob Geldof and other musicians are once again asking fans to attend a major benefit event. Beginning this Saturday, millions of people in eight cities will be called upon to help Africa. But the plan and its purpose are highly controversial.

2005-06-27.title: Experts Doubt C.I.A. Operatives Will Stand Trial in Italy
2005-06-27.abstract: Experts in international law doubt that 13 people linked to CIA who were ordered arrested by Italian judge last week will ever stand trial there; they are presumed to have left Italy; they are charged with illegally seizing Egyptian cleric on Milan street two years ago and flying him to Egypt for questioning; experts say even if Italy seeks extradition, it is inconceivable that United States government would turn over agents who carried out operation authorized by US and meant to combat terrorism (M)

2005-06-27.title: From the Rubble: A City of Old? Or All Shiny and New?
2005-06-27.abstract: Afghan government is struggling to decide whether to modernize Kabul or to preserve its age-old look as fabled city of gardens and fruit orchards; government states that toll from two decades of war has been 63,000 homes destroyed and 60 percent of streets damaged; photo (M)

2005-06-27.title: Ex-Israeli Soldier Guilty of Manslaughter
2005-06-27.abstract: Human rights groups say Israel has killed many unarmed Palestinians and foreigners and that shootings are rarely investigated.

2005-06-28.title: Pakistan's High Court Reviewing Officially Ordered Gang Rape
2005-06-28.abstract: Supreme Court of Pakistan begins hearing case of Mukhtaran Bibi, also referred to as Mukhtar Mai, whose gang rape on orders of village council inspired international outrage and made her symbol of mistreatment of women in Pakistan; she was gang-raped in 2002 as punishment after her 12-year-old brother was accused of having sex with woman from higher-caste tribe; investigators later said charge was cover-up after boy himself was victim of sex crime; case of Bibi's gang rape has undergone series of twists and turns; Pakistani government barred her from traveling to United States, then lifted restriction after American officials condemned it; now Supreme Court will decide whether to uphold death sentences imposed on those who raped her, or let accused go free; photo (M)

2005-06-28.title: Roll Over, Godzilla: Korea Rules
2005-06-28.abstract: South Korea is emerging as pop culture leader of Asia, with South Korean television, movie and music companies and stars increasingly defining what disparate people in East Asia watch, listen to and play; country is now being valued for trendy entertainers and cutting-edge technology; photo (M)

2005-06-28.title: Murbarak Rival in Egypt Pleads Not Guilty to Election Fraud
2005-06-28.abstract: Ayman Nour and his supporters say the trial was intended to blacken his reputation before the official campaign season.

2005-06-28.title: US transport helicopter crashes in Afghanistan
Japanese emperor visits a WWII battleground

2005-06-29.title: Ex-Franco minister loses vote in northwest Spain
France is picked for nuclear fusion site
Hostile fire appears to have downed U.S. Chinook helicopter
Facing Possible Return
2005-06-29.abstract: Petty Officer Second Class Jesus Garcia was happy to be stationed on his couch on Tuesday, his cocker spaniel at his side and his air conditioning humming in the sticky Florida night.    Yet Petty Officer Garcia, who was deployed in Iraq for seven months last year as reservist with Naval Mobile Construction Battalion 14 and who is possibly facing a return, watched approvingly as President Bush said he would not set a timetable for withdrawing troops from Iraq.

Public heartened by president's appraisal of war

Canada moves toward ban on exporting drugs
